_clinical_notes: Groin strain typically involves adductor muscles (most common) or hip flexors (iliopsoas). Grade I-II adductor strains are the majority. Red flags: sharp hip crease pain with clicking/locking = hip labral tear, refer. Groin pain in adolescent males = rule out slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) — severe pain, limp, hip external rotation — seek care. Grade I: mild, full WB. Grade II: moderate, limp. Grade III: complete rupture, severe pain, palpable defect, cannot bear weight.

mechanism.prompt: How did the groin injury happen?

options.label: Kicking, side-stepping, or change of direction
Direct blow to the groin area

location.prompt: Where is most of the pain?

click_check.prompt: Do you notice any clicking, catching, or locking sensation in your hip?

options.label: Yes, clicking or catching in the hip

pop_groin.prompt: Did you feel a pop or tearing sensation at the moment of injury?

options.label: Yes, a clear pop or tear

function_groin.prompt: How is your walking ability right now?

options.label: Very painful to walk / cannot bear weight

outcome.seek_care_reason: Clicking or catching in the hip joint can indicate a hip labral tear, which needs a proper evaluation and imaging to diagnose.
Severe groin pain with inability to bear weight may indicate a significant muscle rupture or hip injury. You should be evaluated.
outcome.injury_subtype: Severe Groin / Adductor Strain - Grade III
